description: 'lila' introduces an intended outcome. English often renders it as in order to, so that, or as to. Unlike 'lao' (BECAUSE), it relates an action to an agent's purpose; an unintended result takes causal wording instead.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and let voiced air escape along both sides as the tongue rises into high-front unrounded 'i'. Repeat the lateral contact, then lower the jaw into open central 'a'. Stress 'li'.
usage_notes: The purpose clause normally follows the action it explains. In the construction 'S lila V ralu nai', the form frames an action as the respect in which the subject is free; this is Phi's regular expression of 'need not'.

examples.translation: I read in order to learn.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Right Intention asks how an aim relates to an action. 'lila' places that aim beside the action in the clause itself.
